intro: Migrating C# to JavaScript shifts logic from a compiled, multi-threaded enterprise architecture (.NET) to a dynamic, single-threaded event-driven environment native to the web ecosystem. Developers trade explicit structural interfaces, type safety, and classical inheritance for highly flexible prototypes, dynamic objects, and non-blocking asynchronous I/O loops. This transition facilitates universal browser/server execution but introduces runtime safety challenges.

diffs.from: Static, nominal type layout requiring explicit definitions, interface compliance, and rigid generic type bounds.
diffs.to: Dynamic, weak type configuration relying on loose coercion, runtime evaluation, and prototype assignments.

diffs.from: Strict class structures requiring access modifiers (public/private), namespace blocks, and mandatory compilation entry points.
diffs.to: Flexible, multi-paradigm layout supporting functional coding, first-class closure bindings, and class abstractions without rigid type schemas.

diffs.from: Optimized .NET garbage collection scheme separating execution stacks from generational garbage collector heap layers.
diffs.to: V8 engine or target host runtime tracing garbage collector managing all dynamic structures and lexical closure scopes.

diffs.from: Task Parallel Library processing routines operating asynchronously across system hardware thread allocations.
diffs.to: Single-threaded execution loop using a concurrency model based on an event loop, Promises, microtask queues, and async/await mechanics.

diffs.from: Enormous platform base class library containing low-level IO, concurrent collections, and robust cryptography systems.
diffs.to: Minimal global runtime scope providing primitive manipulations, basic array mutations, and web platform API extensions.

examples.title: Object Structure and Mapping
examples.description: Compares class-based projection in C# against direct dynamic object modification and map iteration in JavaScript.

examples.title: Promise Delay Execution
examples.description: Shows mapping C# Task delays to JavaScript native Promise wrappers using async/await syntax structures.

tips.title: Guard Against Dynamic Coercion
tips.body: JavaScript automatically coerces conflicting variable types during logic checks. Always use strict equality operators (`===` and `!==`) instead of abstract equality (`==`) to replicate C# comparison rules safely.

tips.title: Embrace First-Class Functions
tips.body: Do not build heavy class hierarchies just to pass behavior. JavaScript supports first-class functions; pass functions directly into variables or callbacks without routing through interfaces or delegates.

tips.title: Isolate Blocking Code Blocks
tips.body: Long-running computational loops block JavaScript's single thread completely, freezing user interfaces. Offload heavy numerical processes or complex data parsers to background Web Workers.

tips.title: Use Linters and Modern Tooling
tips.body: Because JavaScript does not check types at compile time, use `ESLint` configurations along with strict runtime mode (`"use strict";`) to catch syntax typos and scope leaks early in development.
